_meta.description: Expanded question bank for CyberAI. 75 questions per module (30 original + 45 new). At runtime: shuffle the pool per module, pick 15, randomize option order.

phishing.question: After identifying a suspicious email as a phishing attempt, what is the recommended next step?
options: Forward it to all your colleagues to warn them.
Mark it as spam and delete it.
Reply to the sender telling them you know it's a scam.
Report it to your IT/Security department using the official reporting channel.
phishing.explanation: Reporting phishing to IT/Security lets them analyze the email, block the sender, and protect the whole organization. Marking as spam or deleting is insufficient.

phishing.question: What should you do if you accidentally click a link in a phishing email?
options: Immediately restart your computer.
Disconnect from the internet and report it to IT/Security right away.
Delete the email from your inbox and trash.
Run an antivirus scan and hope for the best.
phishing.explanation: Disconnecting can stop malware spreading or 'phoning home'. Reporting immediately lets the security team act to protect you and the company.

phishing.question: Which of these is the BIGGEST red flag for a phishing email?
options: The email contains the company's official logo.
The sender's email address is from a public domain (like @gmail.com) but claims to be a bank.
The email is addressed to you by your first name.
The email offers you a discount on a future purchase.
phishing.explanation: Legitimate businesses email from their official corporate domain, not a free public one. A mismatched sender domain is a very strong phishing indicator.

phishing.question: You receive an email from 'Netflix' with the subject 'Your account is on hold'. It asks you to click a link to update your payment details. What is the safest course of action?
options: Click the link and enter your details; you don't want to miss your shows.
Ignore the email and assume it's a mistake.
Open a new browser window, go to the official Netflix website, and check your account status there.
Reply to the email asking for confirmation.
phishing.explanation: Never trust links in unexpected account-security emails. Go directly to the official website through a trusted method to verify any claims.

phishing.question: What does 'spear phishing' refer to?
options: Phishing that targets a specific individual using personalized information.
Phishing sent to millions of random addresses.
Phishing that only happens over the phone.
A phishing email with a fish-themed logo.
phishing.explanation: Spear phishing is highly targeted, using personal details (name, role, employer) to make the scam far more convincing than mass phishing.

phishing.question: You hover over a link in an email and the status bar shows a different URL than the visible text. What does this most likely indicate?
options: A harmless display glitch.
The link may be disguised to send you to a malicious site.
The email is definitely safe because it has a link.
Your browser needs updating.
phishing.explanation: Mismatched display text and actual URL is a classic phishing trick to hide a malicious destination behind trustworthy-looking text.

phishing.question: Which subject line is the strongest sign of a phishing attempt?
options: Weekly team newsletter
URGENT: Your account will be deleted in 24 hours - verify NOW
Meeting notes from yesterday
Your monthly invoice is attached
phishing.explanation: Manufactured urgency and threats ('verify NOW or lose access') are designed to make you act before thinking. That pressure is a hallmark of phishing.

phishing.question: What is 'whaling' in the context of phishing?
options: Phishing targeting senior executives or high-value individuals.
Sending phishing emails about marine life.
A type of antivirus software.
Phishing that uses very large attachments.
phishing.explanation: Whaling targets 'big fish' such as CEOs and CFOs, often to authorize fraudulent payments or release sensitive data.

phishing.question: An email claims to be from your IT department and asks you to 'confirm your password' by replying. What should you do?
options: Reply with your password since it's the IT department.
Never share your password; legitimate IT will never ask for it this way. Verify via a known channel.
Change your password to something simpler first.
Forward it to a colleague to ask their opinion.
phishing.explanation: Legitimate IT teams never ask for your password by email. Any such request is a credential-harvesting attempt. Verify through a known, official channel.

phishing.question: Which of the following best describes 'clone phishing'?
options: Copying a legitimate email but replacing links/attachments with malicious ones.
Creating a fake social media clone account.
Cloning a hard drive to steal data.
Sending the same email twice by mistake.
phishing.explanation: Clone phishing duplicates a real, previously-sent email and swaps in malicious links or attachments, exploiting the recipient's familiarity with the original.

phishing.question: You get an email with an attachment named 'Invoice_2024.pdf.exe'. What is concerning about it?
options: Nothing, PDFs are always safe.
The double extension hides that it's actually an executable program, not a PDF.
The year in the filename is wrong.
It should be a .docx instead.
phishing.explanation: A double extension like '.pdf.exe' disguises an executable as a document. Running it can install malware. Be extremely cautious of such files.

phishing.question: What is the safest way to verify an unexpected email that appears to be from your bank?
options: Call the phone number provided in the email.
Click the link in the email to log in.
Contact the bank using the number on the back of your card or their official website.
Reply to the email asking if it's real.
phishing.explanation: Always use contact details you obtain independently (card, official site), never the ones provided in a suspicious message, which may route you to the scammer.

phishing.question: Why do phishing emails often contain spelling and grammar errors?
options: The senders are not native speakers, and errors can also filter for less cautious targets.
It makes the email look more professional.
Email systems automatically add errors.
It is required by anti-spam laws.
phishing.explanation: Errors can stem from non-native authors, and some scammers use them deliberately so only less observant people respond, improving their 'yield'.

phishing.question: A pop-up appears saying 'Your computer is infected! Call this number immediately.' What is this?
options: A legitimate Windows security alert.
A scareware/tech-support scam designed to trick you into calling.
A normal browser notification.
Proof your antivirus is working.
phishing.explanation: Real security software does not tell you to phone a number. These pop-ups are scareware aiming to get money or remote access. Close the browser; don't call.

phishing.question: What is 'pharming'?
options: Redirecting users from a legitimate website to a fake one, often via DNS manipulation.
Growing crops with technology.
A method of organic email marketing.
Sending bulk promotional emails.
phishing.explanation: Pharming silently redirects you to a fraudulent site even when you type the correct address, by poisoning DNS or host files, to steal credentials.

phishing.question: You receive a LinkedIn message with a job offer and a link to 'complete your application'. The link goes to an unfamiliar domain. What should you do?
options: Enter your details quickly before the job is taken.
Be cautious; verify the company and recruiter independently before clicking or sharing anything.
Share your bank details so they can set up payroll.
Forward your passwords to confirm your identity.
phishing.explanation: Job-offer phishing is common. Verify the recruiter and company through official channels; never share credentials or financial details via an unsolicited link.

phishing.question: Which practice most reduces the damage if you fall for a phishing attack on one account?
options: Using the same password everywhere for consistency.
Using unique passwords and enabling 2FA on each account.
Writing all passwords in one email to yourself.
Never logging out of accounts.
phishing.explanation: Unique passwords plus 2FA mean a single compromised credential doesn't unlock your other accounts, containing the breach.

phishing.question: An email says you've won a lottery you never entered and must pay a 'processing fee' to claim it. This is:
options: A legitimate prize notification.
An advance-fee scam; legitimate lotteries don't charge winners upfront.
A reason to celebrate and pay quickly.
A standard tax procedure.
phishing.explanation: Advance-fee scams promise a large reward for a small upfront payment that you'll never recoup. You cannot win a lottery you never entered.

phishing.question: What information in an email header can help identify spoofing?
options: The font used in the email body.
The actual sending server and 'Return-Path' compared to the claimed sender.
The number of words in the subject.
The color of the company logo.
phishing.explanation: Header fields like Return-Path and the originating server reveal whether the real source matches the displayed sender, exposing spoofing.

phishing.question: A vendor emails that their bank details have changed and asks you to send the next payment to a new account. What is the correct action?
options: Update the details and pay immediately.
Call the vendor on a previously known, verified number to confirm the change.
Reply to the email to confirm.
Ignore it entirely and never pay the vendor again.
phishing.explanation: Bank-detail-change requests are a top Business Email Compromise tactic. Always verify via a trusted, previously-known phone number before changing payment info.

phishing.question: Which of these is NOT typically a sign of a phishing email?
options: A personalized greeting using your correct full name and recent order number.
Urgent threats of account closure.
Requests for sensitive information.
Mismatched or suspicious sender domains.
phishing.explanation: While not a guarantee of safety, accurate personalization is less typical of mass phishing. Urgency, info requests, and bad domains are classic red flags.

phishing.question: What is the main risk of enabling 'remote content' or images in a suspicious email?
options: It can confirm to the sender that your address is active and tracked.
It makes the email load slower.
It changes your email font.
There is no risk at all.
phishing.explanation: Tracking pixels in remote images can tell the sender your address is live and that you opened the email, marking you for more attacks.

phishing.question: An email from 'support@paypa1.com' asks you to verify your account. What is suspicious here?
options: Nothing, it's the real PayPal.
The domain uses a number '1' to imitate 'paypal' - a lookalike domain.
PayPal never sends emails.
The word 'support' is not allowed.
phishing.explanation: 'paypa1.com' substitutes a '1' for an 'l' to impersonate paypal.com. Lookalike (typosquatting) domains are a common phishing technique.

phishing.question: Why is it risky to use the 'unsubscribe' link in a suspicious spam/phishing email?
options: It always works perfectly and is safe.
It can confirm your address is active or lead to a malicious page.
It will subscribe you to more lists automatically by law.
It deletes your email account.
phishing.explanation: In phishing/spam, 'unsubscribe' links may validate your address to attackers or point to malware. For untrusted senders, mark as spam instead.

phishing.question: What is 'business email compromise' (BEC)?
options: When your business email runs out of storage.
A scam impersonating executives or vendors to trick staff into transfers or data disclosure.
A software bug in email clients.
A type of email backup.
phishing.explanation: BEC uses spoofed or compromised business accounts to impersonate trusted parties and authorize fraudulent payments or leak sensitive information.

phishing.question: A text-only email with no links, from a known colleague, asks you to 'urgently buy gift cards for a client and send the codes'. What should you do?
options: Buy them immediately to help your colleague.
Verify directly with the colleague by phone or in person; gift-card requests are a common scam.
Reply with your manager's email so they can ask them instead.
Send the codes and ask for reimbursement later.
phishing.explanation: Gift-card requests are a classic BEC/social-engineering scam, even without links. Verify out-of-band before acting, regardless of who it appears to be from.

phishing.question: Which email authentication standards help detect spoofed senders?
options: HTTP, FTP, and SSH.
SPF, DKIM, and DMARC.
HTML, CSS, and JS.
JPEG, PNG, and GIF.
phishing.explanation: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC are email authentication mechanisms that help verify a message genuinely came from the domain it claims, reducing spoofing.

phishing.question: You're unsure if an email is phishing. What is the most prudent default action?
options: Click links to test where they go.
Do not click anything; verify through official channels or report to IT.
Forward it to all colleagues to decide together.
Reply asking the sender to prove they're legitimate.
phishing.explanation: When in doubt, don't interact. Verifying independently or reporting to IT avoids triggering malicious links or tipping off attackers.

phishing.question: What makes mobile devices a growing target for phishing?
options: Phones can't get phishing at all.
Smaller screens hide full URLs and sender details, making scams harder to spot.
Phones automatically block all phishing.
Mobile email is encrypted so it's always safe.
phishing.explanation: On mobile, truncated URLs and hidden header details make it easier to miss red flags, so phishing on phones is increasingly effective.

phishing.question: A colleague reports a phishing email that you also received. What is the best team practice?
options: Stay silent so you don't look paranoid.
Report it to IT/Security too, so they can assess scope and block it organization-wide.
Delete yours and forget about it.
Reply to the attacker to warn them you reported it.
phishing.explanation: Multiple reports help security gauge a campaign's reach and respond faster. Reporting (not deleting silently) strengthens organizational defense.

phishing.question: What is 'whaling' in cybersecurity?
options: A large-scale phishing attack sent to thousands of random users
Phishing attacks targeting senior executives or high-value individuals
Phishing emails that impersonate whale-conservation charities
An attack that uses maritime industry themed lures
phishing.explanation: Whaling targets 'big fish' â€” executives like CEOs and CFOs. The high-value targets make these attacks worth the extra personalization effort.

phishing.question: What is a 'clone phishing' attack?
options: Creating a duplicate social media account of a real person
Duplicating a legitimate email and replacing its links or attachments with malicious ones
Cloning someone's voice using AI to call their contacts
Making pixel-perfect copies of login pages
phishing.explanation: Clone phishing takes a real, previously delivered email and replaces legitimate content with malicious links or attachments, then resends it from a spoofed address.

phishing.question: What does 'Business Email Compromise' (BEC) typically involve?
options: Sending bulk spam to corporate email addresses
Impersonating an executive or trusted partner to authorize fraudulent wire transfers
Compromising a company's email server to send ransomware
Installing keyloggers on business computers via email
phishing.explanation: BEC scams impersonate executives or vendors to trick finance staff into making unauthorized payments. FBI reports billions lost annually to BEC.

phishing.question: Which email authentication standard publishes a list of authorized mail servers for a domain?
options: SSL/TLS
SPF (Sender Policy Framework)
HTTPS
PGP encryption
phishing.explanation: SPF lets domain owners publish which mail servers are authorized to send email on their behalf, helping receiving servers detect spoofed senders.

phishing.question: You receive email from 'support@paypa1.com' (the letter 'l' replaced with '1'). This technique is called:
options: Spoofing
Typosquatting
Pharming
Clone phishing
phishing.explanation: Typosquatting registers domains with slight misspellings of trusted brands. Victims who don't notice the difference hand over credentials to attackers.

phishing.question: Which attachment type should raise the most suspicion in an unexpected email?
options: A PDF from your bank
A macro-enabled Office file (.xlsm) from an unknown sender
A JPEG photo from a colleague
A plain text .txt file
phishing.explanation: Macro-enabled Office files are a top malware delivery vector. Macros can execute code when the file is opened, silently installing malware.

phishing.question: An email warns 'Your account will be deleted in 24 hours unless you verify immediately.' Which psychological trigger is being used?
options: Authority
Scarcity
Urgency
Reciprocity
phishing.explanation: Urgency short-circuits careful thinking. Attackers create artificial time pressure so victims act before they have time to verify the request.

phishing.question: What does DMARC help prevent?
options: Malware attachments in emails
Email domain spoofing and phishing
Spam from mailing lists
Unauthorized email encryption
phishing.explanation: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance) builds on SPF and DKIM to prevent attackers from spoofing your domain in phishing emails.

phishing.question: A URL reads 'paypal.com.login.evil.com'. Which domain is the actual owner of this page?
options: paypal.com
login.evil.com
evil.com
com
phishing.explanation: The real domain is always the last two parts before the first slash. Here that's 'evil.com' â€” 'paypal.com' is just a misleading subdomain.

phishing.question: You receive an unexpected password-reset email you did not request. The best action is:
options: Click the link to see what is changing
Ignore it completely
Go directly to the service website without clicking the link and secure your account, then report the email
Forward it to friends as a warning
phishing.explanation: An unrequested password reset may mean someone is trying to take over your account. Go to the site directly, change your password, and report it to IT.

phishing.question: What distinguishes spear phishing from regular phishing?
options: Spear phishing always uses phone calls instead of email
Spear phishing is personalized with victim-specific details to increase credibility
Spear phishing only targets government agencies
Spear phishing uses encrypted messages
phishing.explanation: Spear phishing researches the target (name, role, colleagues, recent events) to craft highly convincing tailored messages, unlike mass generic phishing.

phishing.question: Which of these is a safe way to verify a suspicious email claiming to be from your bank?
options: Click the link in the email and check if the site looks real
Reply to the email and ask if it is genuine
Call the bank using the number on the back of your card
Google the bank name and use the first link that appears
phishing.explanation: Use a trusted, pre-existing contact method (back of your card, official website typed manually) â€” never a number or link provided in the suspicious message.

phishing.question: What is 'angler phishing'?
options: Phishing via fishing-hobby forums
Exploiting frustrated customers on social media by impersonating brand support accounts
Phishing emails pretending to be from fishing companies
A mass phishing campaign targeting rural communities
phishing.explanation: Angler phishing targets unhappy customers on social media who publicly complain about a brand. Attackers pose as the brand's support team and direct victims to fake help pages.

phishing.question: What does a 'sandbox' environment do to protect against email-borne malware?
options: Stores suspicious emails in a quarantine folder indefinitely
Runs suspicious attachments in an isolated environment to observe malicious behavior safely
Encrypts all incoming email automatically
Blocks all emails containing links
phishing.explanation: Sandboxing detonates suspicious attachments in a contained virtual environment. If malicious behavior is detected, the email is blocked before reaching the user.

phishing.question: Pharming attacks differ from phishing because they:
options: Are carried out entirely over the phone
Redirect users to fake websites even when they type the correct URL
Only target mobile devices
Send thousands of emails instead of targeted ones
phishing.explanation: Pharming corrupts DNS or the hosts file to redirect legitimate domain requests to attacker-controlled servers. Victims see the right address in the browser but land on a fake site.

phishing.question: Which of the following is NOT a reliable indicator that an email is legitimate?
options: The email was expected and specifically requested by you
The sender's domain exactly matches the official company domain
The email contains the company's official logo
The email is addressed to your full name
phishing.explanation: Logos are trivial to copy from public websites. Attackers routinely include official logos. A logo alone proves nothing about an email's authenticity.

phishing.question: What is 'pretexting' in the context of phishing?
options: Adding misleading text in email subject lines
Fabricating a believable scenario (pretext) to manipulate someone into divulging information
Using pre-written text templates for phishing emails
Writing emails with large amounts of invisible text
phishing.explanation: Pretexting creates a false context â€” e.g., 'I'm calling from IT about your ticket' â€” to make the request feel legitimate and lower the victim's guard.

phishing.question: A colleague sends you a link over Teams saying 'Check this out!' with no other context. You should:
options: Click it immediately â€” it came from a trusted colleague
Message your colleague through a separate channel to confirm they actually sent it
Only click if your antivirus is running
Click and check if the page looks suspicious before entering any data
phishing.explanation: Colleagues' accounts can be compromised. Always verify unexpected links out-of-band (call, separate chat) before clicking, especially from a message with no context.

phishing.question: What is 'email header analysis' used for?
options: Improving email subject line effectiveness
Examining metadata in an email to verify its true origin and detect spoofing
Formatting email content for better display
Compressing email attachments
phishing.explanation: Email headers contain routing information including the originating IP and mail servers. Security teams analyze headers to trace phishing emails back to their real source.

phishing.question: Which action MOST effectively protects an organization from phishing?
options: Buying the most expensive firewall
Conducting regular phishing simulation training for all employees
Setting email to plain-text only
Never using email for sensitive communications
phishing.explanation: Human awareness is the primary defense against phishing. Regular simulations train employees to recognize and report attacks, reducing the organization's risk dramatically.

phishing.question: A phishing email achieves its goal when:
options: The victim opens the email
The victim marks the email as spam
The victim takes the intended action (clicks link, opens attachment, submits credentials)
The email bypasses the spam filter
phishing.explanation: Simply opening a phishing email (in modern clients) rarely causes harm. The attack succeeds when the victim performs the intended action: clicking a link, entering credentials, or opening a malicious attachment.

phishing.question: What is 'quishing'?
options: Quiet phishing where no email is sent
Phishing using QR codes that direct users to malicious websites
Quick phishing attacks that last under a minute
Phishing attacks that target quiz platforms
phishing.explanation: Quishing (QR phishing) embeds malicious URLs in QR codes. Since QR content isn't human-readable, victims can't inspect the link before scanning, making detection harder.

phishing.question: Which of these sender addresses is the most suspicious for an email claiming to be from Microsoft?
options: support@microsoft.com
noreply@microsoft.com
security@microsoft-helpdesk.net
admin@microsoft.com
phishing.explanation: 'microsoft-helpdesk.net' is a different domain from 'microsoft.com'. Legitimate Microsoft emails come from @microsoft.com subdomains, not third-party look-alike domains.

phishing.question: What makes a 'multi-stage phishing attack' particularly dangerous?
options: It requires multiple attackers working simultaneously
It gradually builds trust through several interactions before making the malicious request
It sends multiple emails to the same victim in rapid succession
It simultaneously attacks multiple organizations
phishing.explanation: Multi-stage attacks invest time to build credibility â€” weeks of normal-looking communication â€” before the actual attack. By then the victim has significant trust in the attacker.

phishing.question: How can you tell a padlock icon in the browser address bar?
options: It guarantees the website is legitimate and trustworthy
It only means the connection is encrypted, not that the site is legitimate
It means the website has been verified by the government
It means no malware is present on the site
phishing.explanation: HTTPS/padlock only confirms the connection is encrypted. Phishing sites routinely obtain valid SSL certificates, so a padlock does NOT mean the site is safe.

phishing.question: Which of these is a common phishing email subject line tactic?
options: 'Monthly newsletter from your favourite brand'
'Action required: Verify your account immediately'
'Thank you for your recent order'
'Welcome to our community'
phishing.explanation: Urgency-loaded subject lines ('Action required', 'Immediate', 'Alert') are designed to trigger panic and bypass rational thinking before the email is even opened.

phishing.question: What is 'email harvesting'?
options: Filtering out spam from your inbox
Automatically collecting valid email addresses from websites, social media, and breached databases to build target lists for phishing campaigns
Archiving old emails to free up mailbox space
A legitimate marketing tool for gathering subscriber emails
phishing.explanation: Attackers use scrapers to collect email addresses from public sources. These lists are used directly for phishing or sold to other threat actors. Obfuscating your email address on public sites reduces harvesting risk.

phishing.question: A friend's email account sends you a message saying 'Check out this link!' with no context. What should you do?
options: Click the link â€” it came from a friend's real email address
Do not click â€” the friend's account may be compromised; contact them via another channel to verify
Click the link but only if you are on a secure network
Reply to the email asking if they meant to send it
phishing.explanation: Compromised email accounts are used to send phishing links to all contacts. The fact that it came from a known address is exactly what makes this effective â€” and suspicious when there is no context.

phishing.question: What is 'geofenced phishing'?
options: Phishing attacks that only target users in specific geographic locations, often to stay below reporting thresholds or target specific regulations
Phishing emails that contain map images to look legitimate
A type of phishing that only works on GPS-enabled devices
Phishing attacks coordinated across multiple countries simultaneously
phishing.explanation: Geofenced attacks deliver malicious content only to visitors from specific countries, appearing harmless to researchers and sandboxes outside the target region, making analysis and takedown harder.

phishing.question: What does it mean when an email passes SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks?
options: The email is definitively safe and from a legitimate sender
The email was sent from an authorized server for that domain â€” but does not guarantee the content or intent is legitimate
The email has been manually reviewed by a human security analyst
The email was encrypted during transmission
phishing.explanation: Passing authentication checks only verifies the sending infrastructure. A legitimate domain can still be used to send phishing (if the domain owner is malicious) or the email can contain deceptive content targeting the recipient.

phishing.question: Why do phishing emails often contain deliberate spelling mistakes?
options: Attackers are not native English speakers and make errors unintentionally
Mistakes help bypass spam filters that rely on exact pattern matching, and also pre-qualify victims â€” those who miss spelling errors are more likely to proceed
Spelling mistakes make the email look more personal and genuine
Email spam filters ignore emails with more than five spelling mistakes
phishing.explanation: Deliberate errors serve two purposes: evading spam filter keywords and filtering for more gullible targets. If a target notices and rejects the email due to errors, they were unlikely to be fooled anyway.

phishing.question: What is 'vishing callback phishing' (telephone-oriented attack delivery)?
options: A phishing email that instructs victims to call a fake support number, where a human operator then conducts the actual social engineering
An automated voice message that leaves a phishing link via voicemail
A combination of video and voice phishing attack
A vishing attack that calls back repeatedly until the victim answers
phishing.explanation: Callback phishing bypasses email security tools by containing no malicious links. The email instructs victims to call a number. A live operator handles the social engineering, making it harder to automate defenses against.

phishing.question: What is the purpose of a 'tracking pixel' in a phishing email?
options: To make the email visually appealing with embedded images
A tiny invisible image that, when loaded, confirms the email was opened and can reveal the recipient's IP address, email client, and approximate location
A pixel that changes colour when the email is forwarded
A security feature that tracks which employees open phishing training emails
phishing.explanation: Tracking pixels confirm delivery and opening, giving attackers verified active targets for follow-up attacks. They can also reveal organizational information through IP addresses.

phishing.question: What is 'link shortening' used for in phishing emails?
options: To make long URLs fit in SMS messages legitimately
To hide the actual malicious destination URL, making it harder for recipients and security tools to assess whether the link is dangerous
To improve the click-through rate of legitimate marketing emails
To bypass rate limits on email servers
phishing.explanation: Shortened URLs (bit.ly, tinyurl) obscure the destination. You cannot evaluate safety without expanding the link first. Hover before you click to see the real URL â€” or use a link expander service to check safely.

phishing.question: An email offers you a tax refund and asks you to enter your bank details to receive it. Which response is correct?
options: Fill in the details if the email looks official and contains your correct name and address
Tax authorities never notify refunds via unsolicited email â€” this is a phishing scam; visit the official tax authority website directly to check your account
Reply to the email asking for more details before providing bank information
Call the number in the email to verify before submitting details
phishing.explanation: Tax refund phishing is a perennial classic. Tax authorities contact by post, not unsolicited email. Any email claiming an unexpected refund with a link or form is a phishing attempt to steal banking credentials.

phishing.question: What is 'domain fronting' in the context of phishing?
options: Placing a company's domain in the email's 'From' display name while using a different actual address
A technique to route malicious traffic through legitimate CDNs to disguise command-and-control communications and bypass network filtering
A method of forging a company's homepage to look identical
Making a fake domain appear first in Google search results
phishing.explanation: Domain fronting routes traffic through major CDN providers (Google, Cloudflare) so network monitors see a trusted domain while the actual destination is malicious. It's used by sophisticated attackers to evade filtering.

phishing.question: What is the 'watering hole' variant of phishing?
options: Sending phishing emails to everyone in a company simultaneously
Compromising a specific website the target group is known to visit and embedding malware there
Creating fake water cooler conversation in company chat to extract information
A phishing attack that uses water utility company branding
phishing.explanation: Rather than reaching out to targets, watering hole attackers compromise sites the target group already visits. Security researchers, industry professionals, or employees visiting familiar sites encounter the attack without any phishing email.

phishing.question: You receive an email asking you to confirm your Google account by logging in via a link. How do you safely verify if this is legitimate?
options: Log in via the link and check if it redirects you to the real Google dashboard
Open a new browser tab and navigate directly to myaccount.google.com to check for any real notifications
Reply to the email and ask Google to confirm they sent it
Look at the lock icon to verify the login page uses HTTPS
phishing.explanation: Phishing pages can have HTTPS and look identical to Google's login. The only safe approach is to independently navigate to the real website without using any links from the suspicious email.

phishing.question: What is 'consent phishing' (OAuth phishing)?
options: Phishing that tricks victims into consenting to data collection policies
Tricking users into granting malicious applications OAuth access to their accounts (email, calendar, contacts) by making the permission request look legitimate
A phishing technique that collects user data with their consent through fake surveys
A GDPR-compliant form of targeted advertising
phishing.explanation: OAuth phishing grants a malicious app access to your Google or Microsoft account without stealing your password. Victims click 'Allow' thinking they're logging into a service. The attacker then has persistent API access without needing credentials.

phishing.question: What is the first thing to check in a suspicious email before clicking anything?
options: The subject line content
The full sender email address (not just the display name)
Whether the email has any images
The number of recipients on the email
phishing.explanation: Display names can be anything â€” 'PayPal Support' can be set by anyone. The actual email address (sender@domain.com) is the only indicator you can initially evaluate for legitimacy without clicking anything.

phishing.question: How do email filters fail to catch sophisticated phishing emails?
options: Email filters only work on weekdays
Sophisticated phishing uses legitimate domains, passes authentication checks, has no malicious URLs (uses phone callback), and employs language and formatting that mirrors real emails from that brand
Email filters cannot read HTML content
Sophisticated phishing attacks only target non-technical users who do not use email filters
phishing.explanation: Modern spear phishing is designed to be indistinguishable by technical controls. This is why human training and verification processes are essential â€” technology cannot fully compensate for sophisticated targeted attacks.

phishing.question: What is 'adversary-in-the-middle' (AiTM) phishing?
options: A type of phishing that targets network administrators
A proxy server intercepts authentication between victim and real site, capturing session cookies in real time â€” allowing account takeover even with MFA active
A phishing attack that places the attacker between two victims
A firewall bypass technique used in phishing campaigns
phishing.explanation: AiTM phishing proxies authentication sessions in real time. The victim logs into the real site (including MFA) through the attacker's proxy. The attacker captures the valid session cookie, bypassing MFA entirely.

phishing.question: Why is hovering over a link before clicking it a good practice?
options: Hovering charges the link with mouse energy, making it safer to click
The status bar shows the actual destination URL, allowing you to spot mismatched domains, IP addresses, or shortened URLs before committing to the click
Hovering delays the click by one second, giving security software time to scan
Hovering notifies your IT department that you are about to click a suspicious link
phishing.explanation: Most email clients and browsers show the real URL in the status bar on hover. This is the simplest way to detect URL mismatch â€” where display text says 'paypal.com' but the actual link goes elsewhere.

phishing.question: What is 'multi-factor phishing resistance' and which MFA type provides it?
options: Using multiple different passwords for the same account
MFA that cannot be bypassed by a phishing attack â€” hardware security keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn) bind authentication to the specific domain, so a phishing site cannot capture a usable credential
Using two different email providers for two-factor authentication
Requiring three factors of authentication for all accounts
phishing.explanation: FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys are cryptographically bound to the exact domain. Even if a phishing site captures your key interaction, it cannot reuse that response on the real site â€” making them the only truly phishing-resistant MFA.

phishing.question: What organizational process helps catch phishing emails that bypass technical filters?
options: Automated deletion of all emails from external senders
Cultivating a reporting culture where employees are rewarded for reporting suspicious emails, combined with a one-click reporting button
Making all employees individually responsible for detecting phishing
Installing antivirus on all devices but not training employees
phishing.explanation: A one-click reporting mechanism and positive reinforcement culture mean each reported email helps the security team update filters and alert the organization to active campaigns. Human reporting catches what technology misses.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the most effective way to manage unique, complex passwords for all your accounts?
options: Write them down in a notebook.
Use a trusted password manager application.
Use the same password everywhere but change it monthly.
Use variations of your pet's name.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Password managers securely create, store, and autofill unique strong passwords for every account, eliminating reuse and memorization.

passwords-2fa.question: Which password example follows best practices for strength and uniqueness?
options: YourCompany123
P@sswordForWork!
aPpLePi3&C0ff33!
QwertY@123
passwords-2fa.explanation: 'aPpLePi3&C0ff33!' is long, complex, and avoids common patterns, making it highly resistant to cracking; the others use predictable patterns.

passwords-2fa.question: Which of the following is the WEAKEST password?
options: Fg#9k$2(j@1Po!
Summer2024!
MyDogFido123
Tr0ub4dor&3
passwords-2fa.explanation: 'Summer2024!' follows a predictable word+year+symbol pattern that automated tools guess quickly, unlike a truly random string.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the primary benefit of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)?
options: It makes your password stronger.
It prevents your password from being stolen.
It adds a second layer of security, making your account inaccessible even if your password is stolen.
It automatically logs you into your accounts.
passwords-2fa.explanation: 2FA requires a second factor in addition to your password, so a stolen password alone can't grant access.

passwords-2fa.question: Which 2FA method is generally considered the MOST secure for everyday users?
options: SMS text message codes.
Hardware security keys (e.g., FIDO2/U2F).
Security questions.
Email codes.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Hardware security keys resist phishing and SIM-swap attacks, making them stronger than SMS, email, or knowledge-based factors.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is SMS-based 2FA weaker than an authenticator app?
options: SMS is slower to arrive.
SMS can be intercepted via SIM swapping or SS7 attacks.
Authenticator apps cost money.
SMS codes are longer and harder to type.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Attackers can hijack your number through SIM swapping or exploit telecom flaws to intercept SMS codes; app-based TOTP isn't tied to your phone number.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'passphrase' and why can it be strong?
options: A single short word; it's strong because it's memorable.
A sequence of several random words; length makes it hard to crack yet easy to remember.
Your name plus your birth year.
A password written in another language.
passwords-2fa.explanation: A multi-word passphrase like 'correct-horse-battery-staple' gains strength from length and entropy while remaining memorable.

passwords-2fa.question: What does 'credential stuffing' exploit?
options: Weak Wi-Fi passwords.
Password reuse across multiple sites.
Outdated antivirus software.
Physical access to a computer.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Credential stuffing takes leaked username/password pairs and tries them on other sites, succeeding wherever people reused the same password.

passwords-2fa.question: You receive a 2FA code you didn't request. What does this likely mean?
options: A harmless system test.
Someone may have your password and is trying to log in - change it immediately.
Your phone is malfunctioning.
You should share the code to cancel it.
passwords-2fa.explanation: An unrequested code suggests someone is attempting to log in with your password. Don't share the code; change your password and review account security.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the main weakness of using security questions like 'What is your mother's maiden name?'
options: They are too long to answer.
Answers are often public, guessable, or found on social media.
They expire too quickly.
They require special hardware.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Knowledge-based answers are frequently discoverable through research or social media, making security questions a weak authentication factor.

passwords-2fa.question: What does 'TOTP' stand for in authenticator apps?
options: Time-based One-Time Password.
Total Online Theft Prevention.
Two-Operator Transfer Protocol.
Temporary Office Token Pass.
passwords-2fa.explanation: TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) generates short-lived codes that change every 30 seconds, used by apps like Google Authenticator and Authy.

passwords-2fa.question: How does a password manager help protect against phishing sites?
options: It emails you warnings.
It only autofills credentials on the exact matching legitimate domain.
It blocks all websites by default.
It changes your password every hour.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Because managers match the stored domain precisely, they won't autofill on a lookalike phishing domain, giving you a silent warning that something's wrong.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'brute-force' attack on a password?
options: Physically forcing open a laptop.
Systematically trying many combinations until the correct one is found.
Calling the user to ask for it.
Guessing based on a single hint.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Brute force tries enormous numbers of combinations. Length and complexity exponentially increase the time required, defeating the attack.

passwords-2fa.question: Why should you avoid reusing your email password on other sites?
options: Email passwords are special and can't be reused technically.
Your email can reset other accounts, so its compromise can cascade everywhere.
It slows down your inbox.
Reuse is fine if the email is from a big provider.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Your email is the recovery hub for most accounts. If its password leaks and is reused, attackers can reset and seize many other accounts.

passwords-2fa.question: What are 'passkeys' designed to do?
options: Replace passwords with cryptographic keys tied to your device and biometrics.
Store passwords in plain text.
Generate longer passwords automatically.
Share passwords between friends.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Passkeys use public-key cryptography unlocked by device biometrics/PIN, removing shared secrets and resisting phishing and credential theft.

passwords-2fa.question: A website forces a 90-day password change with no other reason. Modern guidance says:
options: Frequent forced rotation is always best.
Forced periodic changes can lead to weaker, patterned passwords; change on evidence of compromise instead.
Never change passwords under any circumstances.
Change passwords daily for safety.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Current guidance (e.g., NIST) discourages arbitrary forced rotation, which often yields predictable variants. Change when there's a real risk of compromise.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the risk of saving passwords directly in your browser on a shared computer?
options: No risk; browsers are fully secure.
Anyone using that profile may view or use the saved passwords.
It makes websites load slower.
It automatically shares them online.
passwords-2fa.explanation: On a shared or unlocked profile, stored browser passwords can be viewed or used by others. Use a dedicated manager and never save on shared machines.

passwords-2fa.question: What does it mean if a service stores passwords using 'hashing and salting'?
options: They keep your password in plain text.
Passwords are transformed irreversibly with a unique salt, so leaks are far harder to crack.
They text you the password.
They store passwords in a spreadsheet.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Hashing converts passwords into irreversible values; a unique salt per user prevents precomputed (rainbow table) attacks, protecting leaked data.

passwords-2fa.question: You must share access to an account with a teammate. What is the safest approach?
options: Text them the password.
Use the platform's role/permission features or a password manager's secure sharing.
Write it on a sticky note on their monitor.
Email it with the subject 'PASSWORD'.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Native team permissions or a manager's encrypted sharing avoid exposing the raw secret and let you revoke access cleanly later.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'multi-factor authentication' (MFA) compared to 2FA?
options: They are unrelated.
MFA uses two or more factors; 2FA is MFA with exactly two.
MFA is weaker than 2FA.
MFA only means using two passwords.
passwords-2fa.explanation: MFA means two or more independent factors (something you know/have/are). 2FA is the specific case of exactly two factors.

passwords-2fa.question: Which is the strongest combination of authentication factors?
options: A password and a security question.
A password (know) and a hardware key (have).
Two different passwords.
A username and a password.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Combining different factor types - something you know plus something you have - is far stronger than two of the same type, like two knowledge factors.

passwords-2fa.question: What should you do if a service you use announces a data breach?
options: Nothing, breaches don't matter.
Change that password (and anywhere it was reused) and enable 2FA.
Delete your computer.
Wait a year before acting.
passwords-2fa.explanation: After a breach, change the affected password immediately, update any reuses of it, and enable 2FA to limit attacker access.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is password length generally more important than adding one special character?
options: Special characters are illegal.
Each added character multiplies the possible combinations far more than symbol tricks on a short password.
Length doesn't matter at all.
Symbols slow down typing.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Entropy grows with length; a long passphrase typically beats a short password with a few symbols, which attackers' rules anticipate.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'dictionary attack'?
options: Reading a physical dictionary.
Trying common words and known passwords rather than every random combination.
Translating passwords between languages.
An attack on library systems only.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Dictionary attacks try lists of common words, leaked passwords, and patterns - which is why predictable, word-based passwords fall fast.

passwords-2fa.question: Where is the SAFEST place to store your password manager's master password?
options: In a text file on your desktop.
Memorized (and optionally a copy in a secure offline location like a safe).
On a sticky note on your laptop.
In an email to yourself.
passwords-2fa.explanation: The master password should be memorized; if you keep a backup, store it offline and physically secured, never in a digital file or email.

passwords-2fa.question: An app offers 'Login with Google/Apple'. What is a security benefit of this?
options: It shares your Google password with the app.
You avoid creating another password, leveraging the provider's strong auth and 2FA.
It removes the need for any security.
It makes your account public.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Federated login means no new password to leak; you rely on a well-secured provider account (ideally with 2FA) rather than the app's own store.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'SIM swapping' and why does it threaten SMS 2FA?
options: Switching phone cases.
An attacker ports your number to their SIM to receive your SMS codes.
Upgrading your data plan.
Swapping apps between phones.
passwords-2fa.explanation: In SIM swapping, attackers trick the carrier into moving your number to their device, intercepting SMS codes and bypassing SMS-based 2FA.

passwords-2fa.question: Which habit most improves your overall account security with least effort?
options: Changing passwords daily.
Using a password manager plus 2FA on important accounts.
Memorizing one strong password for everything.
Disabling all logins.
passwords-2fa.explanation: A manager (unique strong passwords) combined with 2FA on key accounts gives the biggest security gain for minimal ongoing effort.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the danger of an account with a strong password but NO 2FA if the site is breached?
options: None, a strong password is always enough.
If the password leaks or is phished, there's no second barrier to stop access.
The password becomes stronger automatically.
The site will reset it for you instantly.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Without 2FA, a leaked or phished password is the only barrier. 2FA adds a second factor that protects you even if the password is exposed.

passwords-2fa.question: You're setting up 2FA and the site offers 'backup/recovery codes'. What should you do with them?
options: Ignore them; you won't need them.
Store them securely offline so you can regain access if you lose your device.
Post them in a group chat.
Use them as your new password.
passwords-2fa.explanation: Recovery codes let you back in if you lose your 2FA device. Store them securely and offline; never share them or use them as a password.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'dictionary attack' on passwords?
options: An attacker searches a physical dictionary for password hints
Automated attempts using common words, phrases, and variations as passwords
A social engineering attack where the attacker talks to you
An attack that targets your browser's saved passwords
passwords-2fa.explanation: Dictionary attacks use wordlists of common passwords, words, and known patterns. Using a random passphrase from multiple unrelated words significantly reduces this risk.

passwords-2fa.question: Which password is the strongest?
options: Password1!
MyDog2019
Tr0ub4dor&3
xK9#mP2@qL8$wN5
passwords-2fa.explanation: Length combined with true randomness is strongest. A 16-character random string with mixed character types has vastly more possible combinations than a humanly memorable pattern.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'credential stuffing'?
options: Filling in all form fields with random data to find vulnerabilities
Using username/password pairs from previous data breaches to try to log into other services
Storing too many passwords in one place
Creating multiple accounts on the same service
passwords-2fa.explanation: People often reuse passwords. Credential stuffing automates login attempts across many services using stolen credentials from one breach to compromise accounts on others.

passwords-2fa.question: Which 2FA method is considered the MOST secure?
options: SMS one-time codes
Email verification codes
Hardware security key (like YubiKey)
Knowledge-based security questions
passwords-2fa.explanation: Hardware keys use cryptographic challenge-response and are phishing-resistant â€” the key only works on the legitimate domain, unlike SMS or TOTP which can be phished.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is SMS-based 2FA considered weaker than app-based TOTP?
options: SMS codes expire too quickly
SMS codes can be intercepted via SIM swapping attacks or SS7 vulnerabilities
SMS codes are longer and harder to type
SMS 2FA doesn't work internationally
passwords-2fa.explanation: SIM swapping (convincing a carrier to transfer your number) and SS7 protocol flaws allow attackers to intercept SMS codes. TOTP app codes never leave your device.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'password manager' and why is it beneficial?
options: A person who manages passwords for an organization
Software that generates, stores, and auto-fills strong unique passwords for every site
A browser feature that remembers only your most recent password
A policy document outlining password rules
passwords-2fa.explanation: Password managers eliminate the need to remember multiple passwords, enabling unique strong passwords for every account â€” the single best practice to prevent credential-based attacks.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'brute force' password cracking?
options: Using social pressure to make someone reveal their password
Systematically trying every possible character combination until the correct password is found
Using extremely fast computers to guess common passwords
Breaking a keyboard to access someone's computer
passwords-2fa.explanation: Brute force tries all possibilities. Strong, long passwords make this computationally infeasible. A 12-character random password would take centuries to brute force with current hardware.

passwords-2fa.question: You receive an SMS code you did not request. What should you do?
options: Enter the code wherever it is needed
Ignore it and continue normally
Immediately secure your account â€” someone is trying to access it using your credentials
Call the number that sent the SMS
passwords-2fa.explanation: An unrequested 2FA code means someone has your password and is attempting to log in. Change your password immediately and check for unauthorized access.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'passkey'?
options: A master password that unlocks all other passwords
A cryptographic credential tied to your device that replaces passwords entirely
A very long password phrase using multiple words
A physical key used to unlock encrypted hard drives
passwords-2fa.explanation: Passkeys use public-key cryptography. Your device holds the private key; the service stores the public key. No password is transmitted, eliminating phishing and credential theft.

passwords-2fa.question: Why should you never reuse passwords across multiple sites?
options: It violates most websites' terms of service
A breach on one site exposes your account on every other site using the same password
Password managers cannot store duplicate passwords
It makes it harder to remember which password you used
passwords-2fa.explanation: Password reuse means one breach cascades into many. Attackers immediately test stolen credentials on popular sites. Unique passwords per site contain the damage to a single breach.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'rainbow table' attack?
options: A colourful visual guide to cracking passwords
Using precomputed hashes to quickly reverse-lookup password hashes without computing them in real-time
An attack that uses multiple attackers simultaneously
A table displaying password complexity rules
passwords-2fa.explanation: Rainbow tables are precomputed tables mapping hashes back to plaintext passwords. They're defeated by 'salting' â€” adding random data to passwords before hashing.

passwords-2fa.question: What does it mean when a website says your password was 'found in a data breach'?
options: Your password is too simple and should be changed
Your exact password has appeared in a known public data breach and attackers may try it
The website has been hacked and you should stop using it
Your email address was used in another website's breach
passwords-2fa.explanation: Services like HaveIBeenPwned check your password against known breach databases. If found, attackers have it and may try credential stuffing. Change it immediately.

passwords-2fa.question: Which is the safest way to store your passwords?
options: In a text file on your desktop named 'passwords.txt'
In a reputable password manager with a strong master password
Written in a notebook kept in your desk drawer
In a spreadsheet protected with a simple password
passwords-2fa.explanation: A password manager encrypts your vault with strong cryptography and requires one strong master password. It's far safer than plaintext files, spreadsheets, or unencrypted documents.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'time-based one-time password' (TOTP)?
options: A password that expires after one hour
A 6-digit code generated by an authenticator app that changes every 30 seconds
A one-use password sent to you by email
A password created at a specific time that never changes
passwords-2fa.explanation: TOTP codes are generated locally by an algorithm using a shared secret and the current time. They expire in 30 seconds, making intercepted codes almost useless.

passwords-2fa.question: When creating a password policy for an organization, which approach is most effective?
options: Require frequent mandatory password changes every 30 days
Require long, complex passwords and encourage password manager use, with changes only after confirmed breach
Require only uppercase and lowercase letters
Allow employees to choose any password they can remember
passwords-2fa.explanation: NIST now recommends against frequent rotation (it leads to weak incremental changes). Long complex passwords changed only when compromised, combined with MFA, is more effective.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'multi-factor authentication' (MFA)?
options: Using multiple passwords for one account
Authentication requiring two or more different categories of evidence: something you know, have, or are
A system that asks multiple security questions
Logging in with multiple email addresses
passwords-2fa.explanation: MFA combines factors from different categories: knowledge (password), possession (phone/key), and inherence (biometrics). Compromising one factor alone is insufficient.

passwords-2fa.question: A hacker has obtained your password from a data breach. With TOTP-based 2FA enabled, can they log into your account?
options: Yes, they can bypass 2FA if they have the password
No, they also need the current TOTP code from your authenticator app
Yes, they can reset the TOTP using your password
No, 2FA can never be bypassed once enabled
passwords-2fa.explanation: 2FA means the attacker needs BOTH your password AND the current TOTP code. Without your authenticator app, the password alone is worthless, significantly improving security.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'security question' and why are they considered weak authentication?
options: A question about security policies â€” they are weak because people don't read policies
Secret questions whose answers are often findable on social media or guessable
A two-step verification method â€” they are weak because they require two steps
IT support tickets â€” they are weak because anyone can submit them
passwords-2fa.explanation: Security question answers (mother's maiden name, pet's name, school) are often public on social media or guessable. They add minimal security and can provide a bypass for real authentication.

passwords-2fa.question: How long should a password be, at minimum, per modern security recommendations?
options: 6 characters
8 characters
12 characters
At least 16 characters
passwords-2fa.explanation: NIST and most modern security guidance recommends at least 16 characters, focusing on length over complexity. Longer passwords are exponentially harder to crack.

passwords-2fa.question: What should you do if you suspect your email account's password has been compromised?
options: Wait to see if anything bad happens
Change the password on that email account and all accounts that use it for password reset
Only change the email password
Log out of all devices and wait 24 hours
passwords-2fa.explanation: Email is the master key â€” most accounts use it for password reset. A compromised email password means all linked accounts are at risk. Change all related passwords immediately.

passwords-2fa.question: Which of the following is an example of 'something you are' in multi-factor authentication?
options: A password
A one-time code sent to your phone
Fingerprint or facial recognition
A hardware security key
passwords-2fa.explanation: 'Something you are' refers to biometrics â€” fingerprint, face scan, iris scan. This is inherence-based authentication, one of the three MFA factor categories.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is it dangerous to use 'autofill' for passwords on shared or public computers?
options: Autofill doesn't work on public computers
The saved credentials can be accessed by other users of that computer
Autofill uses more internet bandwidth
Public computers cannot support autofill
passwords-2fa.explanation: Saved passwords on shared computers are accessible to subsequent users. Always use private/incognito mode and never save credentials on computers you don't own or control.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'pepper' in password security (different from a 'salt')?
options: A password complexity requirement for special characters
A secret value added to passwords before hashing that is stored separately from the database
A type of brute force attack using spicy wordlists
A password manager feature
passwords-2fa.explanation: While a salt is stored alongside the hash, a pepper is a server-side secret not stored in the database. Even if the database is stolen, without the pepper the hashes can't be cracked.

passwords-2fa.question: Your password manager suggests a strong password but you find it difficult to remember. What is the correct approach?
options: Create a simpler, memorable password instead
Use the strong password â€” your password manager remembers it for you
Write the strong password on a sticky note on your monitor
Shorten the password until it is easier to remember
passwords-2fa.explanation: You don't need to remember passwords stored in a password manager. That's exactly its purpose. Use the generated strong password and let the manager handle storage and autofill.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'account takeover' (ATO)?
options: When an administrator takes over an employee's account for IT purposes
When an attacker gains unauthorized access to and control of a legitimate user account
When a user loses access to their own account due to forgetting credentials
When a company acquires another company's user accounts
passwords-2fa.explanation: ATO is when attackers gain control of your account via phishing, credential stuffing, or social engineering. They can then impersonate you, steal data, or launch further attacks.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'password spraying'?
options: Using one very common password (like 'Password1') against many accounts to avoid triggering lockout policies
Spraying encrypted passwords across multiple servers
A technique for distributing passwords securely across an organization
Testing password strength across different categories of users
passwords-2fa.explanation: Password spraying avoids account lockout by trying one common password across thousands of accounts. Unlike brute force (many passwords against one account), it stays below lockout thresholds.

passwords-2fa.question: Why should you avoid using personal information in your password?
options: Personal information makes passwords too long
Information like your name, birthday, or pet's name is often publicly available and will be tried first in targeted attacks
Personal information is prohibited by most websites' password policies
Using personal information makes passwords hard to remember
passwords-2fa.explanation: Attackers performing targeted attacks try personal details first â€” names, birthdays, children's names, hometowns. This information is often available on social media, making these passwords among the weakest choices.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'master password' in the context of password managers?
options: The most important password in your vault
The single strong password that encrypts and unlocks your entire password manager vault â€” the one password you must never forget or compromise
A password shared between multiple administrators
A backup password in case your regular password fails
passwords-2fa.explanation: Your master password is the only key to your entire vault. It should be your strongest, most unique password â€” ideally a long passphrase. If compromised, an attacker has access to every stored credential.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'two-step verification' vs 'two-factor authentication' (2FA)?
options: They are identical terms with no distinction
Two-step verification may use two things from the same factor (two passwords); true 2FA uses different factors (something you know + something you have)
2FA uses technology; two-step verification is a manual process
Two-step verification is for personal accounts; 2FA is for corporate accounts
passwords-2fa.explanation: True MFA requires different categories of evidence. Two steps of the same factor (e.g., two passwords) provides less security than factors from different categories (knowledge + possession + inherence).

passwords-2fa.question: When should you change your password?
options: Every 30 days as a matter of policy
When you have reason to believe it has been compromised, when notified of a breach, when you've shared it, or after using it on a potentially compromised device
Every 90 days as required by most security policies
Only when you forget it and need to reset
passwords-2fa.explanation: NIST's current guidance discourages arbitrary password rotation (it leads to weak patterns like 'Password1!', 'Password2!'). Change passwords specifically when there is evidence of compromise â€” not on a fixed schedule.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'hash cracking' and how does password length affect it?
options: A technique for breaking encrypted disk images â€” length has no effect on encryption strength
Converting a stolen password hash back to plaintext â€” each additional character exponentially increases the time required to crack it
A method of recovering deleted passwords from memory â€” longer passwords take more memory
A network attack that cracks passwords in transit â€” length slows network speeds
passwords-2fa.explanation: Hash cracking works backwards from the hash. Each additional character multiplies the possibilities exponentially. A 16-character password may take millions of years to brute force; an 8-character password may take hours with modern GPU clusters.

passwords-2fa.question: You log into your bank on a hotel computer. What must you do before leaving?
options: Clear the browser history
Log out, clear browsing history and saved passwords, and ideally change your banking password as soon as you are on a trusted device
Only log out â€” clearing data is done automatically
Change your password at the hotel computer before logging out
passwords-2fa.explanation: Shared computers may have keyloggers, session capture tools, or auto-save enabled. Logging out alone is insufficient if credentials were cached. Full clean-up and a password change from a trusted device is the safe approach.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'biometric authentication' and what is its main limitation?
options: Authentication using biological traits (fingerprint, face, iris) â€” main limitation is it requires expensive hardware
Authentication using biological traits â€” main limitation is that biometrics cannot be changed if compromised, unlike passwords
Authentication using behaviour patterns â€” main limitation is high false-positive rates
Authentication using DNA â€” main limitation is processing speed
passwords-2fa.explanation: Unlike passwords, you cannot change your fingerprint. If biometric data is stolen or spoofed, you cannot simply create a new finger. This makes proper storage of biometric templates (never storing raw data) critical.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'push notification MFA' and what is 'MFA fatigue'?
options: Push MFA sends codes via email; fatigue occurs when too many codes are requested
Push MFA sends an approval request to your phone; MFA fatigue is when attackers send repeated push requests hoping the victim approves one out of frustration or confusion
Push MFA requires physical button presses; fatigue refers to button wear
Push MFA is for mobile apps; fatigue happens when the app crashes from overuse
passwords-2fa.explanation: MFA fatigue attacks bombard victims with push notifications until they approve one to stop the disruption. Mitigation includes number matching (requiring the victim to enter a code shown on the login screen into the push notification).

passwords-2fa.question: Why should corporate accounts use different passwords from personal accounts?
options: Corporate policies require different character sets than personal sites
A breach of a personal account (shopping site, forum) could be used to attack the corporate account if the password is the same â€” separate passwords contain the blast radius
Corporate accounts have stronger encryption that requires different passwords
IT departments cannot support personal passwords in enterprise systems
passwords-2fa.explanation: Credential stuffing attacks use personal account credentials against corporate targets. Unique passwords for every account prevent a personal data breach from cascading into corporate network access.

passwords-2fa.question: What is a 'FIDO2 security key' and how does it improve authentication?
options: A physical key that stores your password encrypted
A hardware device using public-key cryptography that authenticates without transmitting secrets â€” the private key never leaves the device, and is cryptographically bound to the specific domain
A key that generates TOTP codes for two-factor authentication
A biometric device that stores fingerprint data for login
passwords-2fa.explanation: FIDO2/WebAuthn creates a unique key pair per site. Authentication requires the private key (which stays in hardware) to sign a challenge. This makes phishing, credential stuffing, and replay attacks impossible.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'password entropy' and why does it matter?
options: The time it takes for a password to be encrypted
A measure of password unpredictability â€” higher entropy means more possible combinations, making guessing and brute force harder
The energy consumed by servers when processing password hashes
The number of characters in a password
passwords-2fa.explanation: Entropy measures randomness. A 20-character password with maximum entropy (truly random characters) is astronomically harder to crack than a 20-character password with low entropy (a sentence or recognizable pattern).

passwords-2fa.question: A site you use announces a data breach and emails you a link to reset your password. What should you do?
options: Click the link in the email and reset immediately
Do not click the link in the email â€” navigate directly to the site yourself to reset your password, as breach notification emails are commonly phished
Only reset if your account was definitely in the breach
Reset your password but use a variation of your current one to remember it easily
passwords-2fa.explanation: Phishers send fake breach notification emails pointing to credential-harvesting pages. Always navigate directly to the affected service to handle the reset, never through links in breach notification emails.

passwords-2fa.question: What is the purpose of 'Bcrypt' or 'Argon2' in password security?
options: These are password managers designed for enterprise use
Deliberately slow hashing algorithms designed to make password cracking computationally expensive â€” even with a stolen database, cracking hashes takes prohibitively long
Fast encryption algorithms for securing passwords in transit
Password generation algorithms that create memorable yet strong passwords
passwords-2fa.explanation: Modern password hashing uses intentionally slow algorithms. This means stolen databases take months or years to crack rather than hours. Fast hashing (MD5, SHA-1) is unsuitable for passwords as modern hardware cracks them quickly.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'identity federation' and how does it relate to password management?
options: A government database of verified identities

Using one trusted identity provider (like Google or Microsoft) for multiple services, reducing passwords â€” convenient but creates a single point of failure if the identity provider account is compromised
A technique for federating (combining) multiple passwords into one master credential
A password policy standard adopted across multiple countries
passwords-2fa.explanation: Single sign-on (SSO) via identity federation reduces password fatigue and enables strong central authentication â€” but compromising the identity provider account gives access to all connected services at once.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is a long passphrase (e.g., 'correct-horse-battery-staple') often stronger than a complex short password like 'P@55w0rd'?
options: Passphrases are easier to type on mobile devices
Length creates far more possible combinations than complexity â€” 4 random words have more entropy than 8 complex characters, and passphrases are both stronger and easier to remember
Complex passwords are harder for users to share with colleagues
Passphrases bypass password filters that reject special characters
passwords-2fa.explanation: 'P@55w0rd' is a predictable pattern that dictionary attacks target. Four random unrelated words create enormous entropy (thousands of trillions of combinations) while being memorable â€” combining security and usability.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'account enumeration' and how does it relate to password attacks?
options: Counting how many accounts a user has across different services
When a system reveals whether a username exists (e.g., 'User not found' vs 'Wrong password'), helping attackers identify valid accounts before launching password attacks
The process of enumerating all passwords in a brute force attack
A technique for identifying all accounts connected to a single email address
passwords-2fa.explanation: Error messages that distinguish between 'username not found' and 'wrong password' help attackers confirm valid usernames before attacking. Secure systems give the same error for both to prevent enumeration.

passwords-2fa.question: What is 'single sign-on' (SSO) and what security consideration should you be aware of?
options: Using the same password for every site â€” a significant security risk
Authenticating once to access multiple connected services â€” convenient and can increase security when backed by strong MFA, but a compromised SSO account grants access to all connected services
A system where one administrator manages all user accounts
A feature that logs you out of all services simultaneously
passwords-2fa.explanation: SSO centralizes authentication risk. The benefit is strong, monitored central authentication; the risk is that one compromised account cascades. SSO is strongest when protected by phishing-resistant MFA.

passwords-2fa.question: What happens to your password manager vault if you lose your master password?
options: Customer support can reset it using your email address
Most reputable password managers use zero-knowledge encryption â€” they cannot recover your vault; losing the master password means losing access permanently unless you have a recovery key
The vault automatically resets after 30 days of inactivity
You can use biometrics to bypass the master password requirement
passwords-2fa.explanation: Zero-knowledge architecture means the service has no way to access your vault â€” it's encrypted with your master password that never leaves your device. This is a security feature, not a bug â€” but it makes backup of the master password critical.

passwords-2fa.question: Why is 'security through obscurity' (using an unusual password format you invented) not a reliable password strategy?
options: It is actually a very reliable strategy â€” unusual formats are hard to guess

Custom obscure formats still follow patterns that attackers model; a truly random password generated by a password manager will always be stronger than a humanly invented format, regardless of how unique it seems
Custom formats are prohibited by most corporate password policies
Security through obscurity requires knowing the attacker's techniques first
passwords-2fa.explanation: Human-created 'unique' patterns (leet speak, reverse spelling, keyboard walks) are all catalogued in attack wordlists. True randomness from a password manager is fundamentally more secure than any pattern a human devises.

malware-ransomware.question: What is the defining characteristic of a ransomware attack?
options: It steals your password.
It slows down your computer.
It encrypts your files and demands a payment for their release.
It displays unwanted pop-up ads.
malware-ransomware.explanation: Ransomware's goal is extortion: it encrypts your files and demands a ransom, usually in cryptocurrency, for the decryption key.

malware-ransomware.question: Why is it crucial to keep your operating system and applications updated?
options: To get new features and a better user interface.
To fix security vulnerabilities that malware can exploit.
To ensure compatibility with new websites.
To make your software run faster.
malware-ransomware.explanation: Updates patch security holes attackers target. Outdated software is a primary vector for malware delivery.

malware-ransomware.question: What is the single most effective way to recover from a ransomware attack without paying the ransom?
options: Unplugging your computer from the internet.
Running an antivirus scan after the attack.
Having recent, secure backups of your important files.
Negotiating with the attackers for a lower price.
